Very few people you're selling to are, what would we, is self-actualized. All right? Got a therapist in the room, so I got to try and get this right. So very few people are self-actualized, right? So what other people think of them is the number one force in their lives, for good and bad. So what they are doing is trying to, in all their mind, they're trying to elevate their status in the eyes of others. So they're not, right? So at a very simplistic level, Brainerd Melanger was delivering elevation of status that until he added the business cards didn't exist, right? Because I get the home study course with my manuals in it on how to be in the import-export business, and I get a catalog of a bunch of stuff I can buy at wholesale, so what? I'm a tree falling in the forest with nobody there to hear it. The business card changes everything, because really, he's in a business card business, okay? So status is a very powerful element in any opportunity pitch. Just as I said earlier, people try not to do things that lower their self-esteem. They're very motivated to do things that raise their self-esteem. So back, all the way back to membership now. If membership raises their status in the eyes of others, membership's a very good thing. If membership stays private and personal and in a drawer, so what? Right? It doesn't help sell the opportunity. It only helps sell the opportunity if it raises their status in the eyes of others. So first of all, so he did the business cards, and he sent the business cards before he delivered the course, right? So three years later, I run into Melanger again, and he says, you want to hear what we're doing now? I said, yeah. He said, you remember the business cards? Remember when I was talking about the business cards? I said, I remember the business cards, right? He said, wait till you see what we're doing now. I said, okay. He said, are you ready? Yeah. So he goes and gets two things, right? First of all, he gets the directory of international traders in which everybody has a page about them, which only goes to the other people by any opportunity. It doesn't matter. It's who's who, right? But he says, here's the thing you have to understand. We send them six copies. He said, it breaks my heart, but we send them six copies. Why? One for them, one for their mother, one for the coffee table, one for, because they want to show them to people. Because everything we do is we send them this big honking thing to put up on their wall. He said, stupid. Nobody ever comes to their wall. That has anything to do with the business. We send them this big honking thing to put up on their wall. So I brought it to show you. So we don't do enough to make a big deal out of the things that deliver value and status that don't require activity or achievement. So for, I think Christmas, I lose track now. But so Carla signs me up for Disney's D23 whiz bang club. Does anybody, a D23 member? One. You got to join. It's bad for me because a lot of shit's delivered online, so she has to print it out and give it to me with a big pin in the ass. But other than that, but what you get, and this is not a lot of money. I forget what it is, but it's not a lot of money. And they got a ton of stuff. They got special events. They got archives. They got different merchandise, whatever. So here's what comes though after you sign up for this. Most of all, this big gigando box comes, which unfolds and there's huge Chip and Dale characters in it. It's this big color coffee table kind of book. There's a D23 wristwatch. There's a D23 pin. The only thing there isn't is a D23 mousier's hat, right? Then a couple days later comes this thing. Now remember, kids ain't signing up in this. It's like three, four hundred bucks a year, right? These are adults mostly, okay? And I get this big honking thing to put up on my wall. Now really? Seriously? Huh? You've already got some background, but I'll give you a little bit before we get started. So George and I have known each other longer than either one of us cares to count. You don't mind me giving your age, do you? You don't care. You already have a hundred times. What's it doing? Okay, so George is like, no, I'm at the specific number. Not the fact that you're, you know, one step away from the grave, I just met the specific number. So George is 76 and is a grand old master of this field, a great student of this field. He and I have known each other a long time. We've had some raging arguments. He built an amazing business. So I will tell you like one representative story. So his offices, if you could call them offices. At one time, employed like 290 some odd people, most of them on the phones, talking to inbound calls mostly from people sent packages about his opportunity. So basically it was one big giant old building in the middle of nowhere. And the two things that stuck in my mind the first time I went there is some of the reps taking calls and making sales, I think because of the speed of growth, were at wall phones on a wall, standing up or sitting on like kitchen stools with full down desks on the wall. The other thing is, is one guy who had an office and I'm not sure the merit system that moved you from the phone in the toilet to the phone to the cubicle to the fire. I don't understand the program.
